patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch removes the collection of a separate stat for wdeleg_getattr, as it is not directly tied to the NFS protocol like other stats. The only difference between wdeleg_getattr and CB_GETATTR is how they handle concurrent GETATTR requests. The patch seeks to simplify the code by removing this redundant stat.

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer argued that the netlink and procfs APIs should remain equivalent, specifically requesting to retain the wdeleg_getattr stat initially, and suggested consulting Dai for further input on its relevance.
review_comments.raw_body:
OK, so you are actually re-litigating whether the stat should exist
at all. I confess I had forgotten that context.

I’m arguing only that the netlink and procfs APIs should remain
equivalent, at least initially. Deleting the stat entirely probably
has no consequences right now, but let’s hear from Dai when the US
west coast wakes up.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: Reviewer questioned the necessity of collecting wdeleg_getattr stat separately, citing its indirect relation to the protocol and suggesting an alternative reporting mechanism through nfsstat.
review_comments.raw_body:
Another option is to keep the stat but report it some other way.
It is arguable whether nfsstat is the proper mechanism to report
the different reasons for sending a CB_GETATTR.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged Chuck's concern that collecting wdeleg_getattr separately was unnecessary, explained the reasoning behind replacing it with CB_GETATTR counts, and confirmed that this change is already implemented in the patch series.

Author acknowledged Chuck's concern about the relevance of collecting wdeleg_getattr stat separately, and decided to add a use-case before deciding whether to count it like before.
review_comments.raw_body:
More or less. IIRC:

We had decided that wdeleg_getattr was sort of an oddity that didn't
really belong in this /proc file. The values in that file are mostly
counting other very specific protocol operations, but wdeleg_getattr is
basically counting GETATTR operations from other clients while there is
an outstanding write delegation on a file.

Since CB_GETATTR counts should approximate wdeleg_getattr, we decided
to start counting those instead (along with all of the other callback
ops). wdeleg_getattr in this series is changed to just report
CB_GETATTR counts.

---

Sure. We could add some way to keep reporting it (or just keep using
/proc for this). AFAIK though, there is no (public) consumer of this
stat. nfs-utils doesn't look at it.

To wit: I'd like to head a clear use-case for this stat before we
decide to we need to count it like we did before.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Chuck Lever
review_comments.summary: reviewer suggested including the wdeleg_getattr statistic in netlink for symmetry and potential future usefulness, despite Jeff's earlier agreement that it was approximately equivalent to CB_GETATTR counter
review_comments.raw_body:
In spite of the email quoting, I’ve still forgotten most of the context
of that conversation.

Looking at this now, I think someone later could get an urge to add the
wdeleg_getattr statistic to netlink… for symmetry, because it smells like
technical debt, or it might actually be useful for something.

IMHO we should include it now.


-- 
Chuck Lever

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author is addressing Chuck Lever's concern about collecting the wdeleg_getattr stat separately, and instead suggests that it's not directly tied to the protocol like other stats. Author questions whether the difference between wdeleg_getattr and CB_GETATTR counters is relevant and asks Dai's opinion on this.
review_comments.raw_body:
IDGI: what's the point of collecting that stat separately when we have
stats for CB_GETATTR already? I'd prefer to avoid perpetuating
wdeleg_getattr as a stat here, since it's not directly tied to the
protocol in the same way as the others are.

The only difference between the two is that if another client does a
GETATTR for the fh while there is already a CB_GETATTR in flight,
wdeleg_getattr gets bumped today, whereas CB_GETATTR counter does not
(since there is only a single CB_GETATTR for that).

Is that difference relevant? I think Dai added wdeleg_getattr
originally, so I'd really like to hear his opinion on this.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 1/3] fuse: fix missing barrier when checking io-uring readiness
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a missing barrier in the FUSE kernel module, which can lead to a CPU observing that io-uring initialization is complete before it actually is. This can cause a deadlock that was previously fixed by commit 3393ff964e0f. The patch adds an smp_rmb() barrier to pair with the existing smp_wmb() in fuse_chan_set_initialized(), ensuring that the two loads are properly ordered and preventing the deadlock.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 2/3] fuse: use release/acquire for fch->initialized
series_items.patch_summary: This patch addresses a potential issue in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) code where lockless readers may access the 'initialized' flag of a connection before it has been properly set by the writer, leading to undefined behavior. To fix this, the patch introduces release/acquire semantics for writing and reading the 'initialized' flag using smp_store_release() and smp_load_acquire(). This ensures that writers and readers are properly synchronized, preventing potential issues. The changes are made in three files: cuse.c, dev.c, and dev_uring.c. The patch is not marked as stable because it addresses a rare edge case that should not occur on well-behaved servers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 3/3] fuse: publish io-uring queues with release semantics
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a potential race condition in the io-uring implementation of the FUSE file system by ensuring that the initialization of fuse_ring_queue structures is properly ordered before their publication. It achieves this by replacing WRITE_ONCE() with smp_store_release() to guarantee that the queue's field initialization happens before its pointer assignment. Additionally, it uses READ_ONCE() for readers accessing the queues to prevent undefined behavior in case of concurrent access.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series fixes several issues related to the initialization of io-uring in the FUSE file system. Specifically, it addresses missing barriers that can cause data corruption and introduces release semantics for publishing io-uring queues. The patches also include some minor cleanups and improvements suggested by reviewers.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v6 0/6] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

version_history.subject: [PATCH v6 0/6] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 1/6] fuse: decouple fuse_ring creation from ent registration
series_items.patch_summary: This patch decouples the creation of fuse_ring from entry registration in FUSE connections. Instead of creating the ring lazily on the first FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_REGISTER command, it is created during connection initialization after the server has negotiated and set FUSE_OVER_IO_URING. This change eliminates the need for open-coded handling to detect and protect against concurrent creations of the fuse_ring. The patch modifies several files in the FUSE codebase to implement this decoupling.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 2/6] fuse: add FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_ADD_QUEUE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_ADD_QUEUE command to the fuse-over-io-uring implementation, allowing servers to create queues explicitly and decoupling queue setup from entry registration. This is necessary for the subsequent addition of buffer pools to existing queues. The patch modifies the fuse_uring_create_queue function to take an additional flag indicating whether to fail if the queue already exists, and updates the fuse_uring_register function to use this new command when creating a queue lazily. The changes also include updates to error handling and logging in various places.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 3/6] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new feature to the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation over io-uring, allowing the server to pass in a buffer pool that the kernel can use for servicing requests. This decouples the relationship between entries and payload buffers, enabling the kernel to optimize memory usage and reduce requirements for using fuse-over-io-uring. The patch adds support for registering a buffer pool per queue with the FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_ADD_BUFPOOL command, which is used by the server to pass in a contiguous chunk of memory. The kernel then splits this region into buffers based on the maximum payload size and tracks free buffers with a bitmap. On dispatch, requests that need a payload claim a free buffer, and on completion, the buffer is returned to the pool or reused directly if needed again.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 4/6] fuse: support registered buffer pools in io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for registered buffer pools in io-uring, allowing servers to use a buffer pool that is also registered through io-uring. This eliminates the overhead of pinning/unpinning user pages and translating virtual addresses per i/o request. The server can register a buffer pool by setting the FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFPOOL flag when adding a bufpool through the FUSE_IO_URING_CMD_ADD_BUFPOOL cmd. The patch includes benchmark results showing improved write performance with registered buffers, bringing it closer to read performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 5/6] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds zero-copy support to the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation, allowing for direct access to client pages or page cache folios without intermediate memory copies. The server can specify which open files should use this feature by setting a flag during opens. This requires administrative privileges and is gated behind CAP_SYS_ADMIN. The patch eliminates unnecessary memory copies between the application, kernel, and server for read/write operations, improving performance.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v6 6/6] docs: fuse: document io-uring buffer pool and zero-copy uapi
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) subsystem, specifically covering its usage of io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy functionality. The new documentation explains how buffer pools decouple entries from payload buffers, allowing for more efficient memory management and reducing overhead. Additionally, it describes zero-copy, a feature that enables direct I/O operations between the server and client's pages without an intermediary copy, requiring CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges. This patch aims to improve understanding of FUSE over io-uring usage and its capabilities.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for io-uring buffer pools to the FUSE file system, allowing servers to pass in a contiguous chunk of memory that the kernel can use to service requests. This decouples the request and payload buffers, reducing memory usage requirements and enabling optimizations for assigning payload memory. Additionally, the series introduces zero-copy functionality, which allows servers with CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges to directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer. The zero-copy feature requires the use of registered bvec changes in io-uring and buffer pools. The patches have been benchmarked to show significant throughput improvements for direct-I/O operations, particularly when backing I/O is not a bottleneck.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH] fuse: disable default bdi strictlimiting
Re: [PATCH v2 3/3] fuse: publish io-uring queues with release semantics
Re: [PATCH v2 1/3] fuse: fix missing barrier when checking io-uring readiness
Re: [PATCH] fuse: disable default bdi strictlimiting
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch reverts a previous change that disabled default bdi (block device interface) strictlimiting, which was intended to prevent the number of dirty pages from exceeding a certain limit. However, it's been found that this change may not be necessary anymore due to subsequent changes in the cgroup locking mechanism, and reverting it resolves performance issues on xfs file systems. The patch suggests that the flush rate limiting might no longer be needed with the current locking scheme, making this revert a simple fix.

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer noted that reverting d9b3ce8769e3 might not be the best solution as it was landed after a change to reduce global lock contention, which could make the flush cost lower and thus render the ratelimiting unnecessary.

Reviewer noted that even with per-subsystem locks, flushing is still expensive due to kernel traversing the cgroup update tree for each CPU on the system, and suggested moving specific stats from rstat to a separate mechanism where updates can be more expensive but reads can be cheap.

Reviewer expressed preference for a more sophisticated rate limiting approach over reverting the patch, suggesting that a general solution should be explored first
review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Jan & Joanne,

On Wed, Jul 15, 2026 at 06:28:16PM -0700, Joanne Koong wrote:

---

Unfortunetely global lock was just part of the cost and even per-subsystem
lock reduces contention across subsystems but flushing is still expensive as the
kernel may have to traverse the cgroup update tree for each cpu on the system.

CCing Yosry who is looking into similar issue but for zswap stats. Maybe a
general solution for such specific stats is to move them from rstat to a
separate mechanism where update can be a bit more expensive but the read side
can be cheap.

---

First let's see if the general solution is doable and simple otherwise we can
explore use-case specific solutions. In extreme case revert is an options as
well but I would prefer a more sophisticated ratelimiting (that Jan suggested)
than a revert.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that reverting the original fix might be sufficient, but will wait for Yosry's work on a more general solution before making any changes.
review_comments.raw_body:
Sounds good. I'll keep an eye out for the work Yosry's doing on this
and look into hooking it up to writeback if/when his general solution
becomes available.

Thanks,
Joanne

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3] mm/huge_memory: unlock i_mmap_rwsem before releasing after-split folios

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug where the i_mmap_rwsem lock is not properly released before freeing after-split folios in the huge_memory module. The issue arises when splitting a THP (Transparent Huge Page) that reaches past the inode's size during truncation, causing a concurrent iput() to evict and RCU-free the inode before the i_mmap_unlock_read() function can touch it. To fix this, the patch moves the i_mmap_unlock_read() call to before the after-split unlock loop, ensuring that all mapping dereferences occur while the folio still pins the inode.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/2] fs/proc/task_mmu: fix PAGEMAP_SCAN written state for PMD holes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the PAGEMAP_SCAN functionality of the Linux kernel, specifically when dealing with PMD holes (huge page table entries) in uffd-wp VMAs (userfaultfd write-protect). The issue arises from pagemap_scan_pte_hole() skipping holes that are not populated with page tables. The patch corrects this by treating unpopulated slots as written, matching the handling of pte_none() in pagemap_page_category(). This ensures that a missing marker in an uffd-wp VMA indicates that the range was cleared via MADV_DONTNEED, preventing data corruption during restore or live migration. The patch excludes hugetlb entries, which are handled differently and do not require this fix.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/2] selftests/mm: add PAGEMAP_SCAN test for THP PMD holes
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new test case to the selftests/mm suite, specifically designed to cover the PMD-hole case fixed by a previous commit. The test creates a MAP_PRIVATE|MAP_ANON THP that is uffd-wp'd and then dropped with MADV_DONTNEED, resulting in a pmd_none hole without a page table. The test verifies that PAGEMAP_SCAN correctly reports this range as written via both the fast and generic query paths. The patch includes necessary header file includes to access MADV_COLLAPSE, which is required for the test. The new test case mirrors an existing unpopulated_scan_test() function, but with modifications to accommodate the THP PMD hole scenario.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/5] mm/memory-failure: keep the folio, not the poisoned subpage, locked across split
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the memory-failure module where a poisoned subpage was being locked across a split operation, leading to a use-after-free error when the inode was evicted while __folio_split() was still running. The fix is to lock and split the folio as a whole, rather than just the poisoned subpage, so that the head of the folio remains locked and pinned in the page cache until the end of the operation.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledges that their original patch is broken due to code expectations and proposes a fix in a different part of the code (2/5), which would change the behavior of memory_failure() and introduce an -EBUSY guard in __folio_split().
review_comments.raw_body:
Right. Non-uniform split can be useful.

But my patch is completely broken because code expects the pin to be on the
@page, not on the head. put_page() few lines down can explode already.

So the fix does not belong in memory_failure(). It belongs in
__folio_split(), and it is really just 2/5: refuse the split with -EBUSY
when @lock_at is at or beyond the sampled EOF.

The safety then sits in __folio_split() regardless of caller or split type,
which should also cover your non-uniform worry.

The behavioural change is that memory_failure() reports a beyond-EOF
poisoned tail as unsplit (MF_FAILED) instead of recovered, and kills the
mappers instead of splitting the page off. What we give up is salvaging
the folio's healthy pages and the clean unmap -- both low value for a page
that is beyond EOF and getting truncated away. Containment is unaffected:
PageHWPoison is set before the split and free_pages_prepare() keeps a
poisoned page out of the buddy allocator, so the bad page never comes back
regardless.

The alternative, if we would rather keep the recovered outcome, is to leave
@lock_at as the poisoned page and move i_mmap_unlock_read() (and
shmem_uncharge()) ahead of the after-split unlock loop, so every mapping
dereference happens while @folio -- the head, within EOF -- still pins the
inode. That is close to Hao's original patch. It works, but it rests on "no
mapping dereference after the unlock loop", which is its own fragility.

I lean towards the -EBUSY guard.

Comments?

-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

review_comments.author: Zi Yan
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Zi Yan suggested an alternative approach to handle the locking of @lock_at, proposing to only use igrab() when @lock_at is at or beyond EOF, and raised concerns about the complexity of determining the index of the after-split folio containing @lock_at for non-uniform splits.

Reviewer Zi Yan noted that the patch introduces another implicit synchronization in the folio split process, requiring documentation of this new requirement, and acknowledged that it works without changing existing behavior.

Reviewer Yan prefers moving i_mmap_unlock_read() before the unlock loop in __folio_split(), but is open to alternative solutions such as returning -EBUSY
review_comments.raw_body:
There is an alternative, only igrab() when @lock_at is at or beyond the EOF,
as I was bouncing ideas with Codex.

Some subtlety exists in these two “@lock_at at or beyond EOF” approaches.
When @lock_at can be a tail page of an after-split folio, Patch 2 returns
-EBUSY unnecessarily, since the after-split folio containing @lock_at will
be still in xarray, preventing inode going away. To make a precise decision,
we will need to determine the index of the after-split folio containing @lock_at
when checking against end. It is easy for uniform split, namely

lock_at_folio_index =
	folio->index + round_down(lock_at - &folio->page, 1UL << new_order);

but for non-uniform split, the calculation is more involved and I have not
figured it out yet.

If we go “return -EBUSY if @lock_at index is at or beyond EOF”, are we OK
with not being able to split a folio when it is actually splittable?

---

This approach works without changing existing behavior, as long as we document
the requirement well.

I admit that this is another implicit synchronization in folio split process,
in addition to
1) keeping original folio frozen until xarray is updated and
2) do not update xarray with after-split folios until after-split folios are
unfrozen.

---

I prefer moving i_mmap_unlock_read() before the unlock loop (shmem_uncharge()
is already before the loop), but I am more than happy to be convinced that
“return -EBUSY” is better or something else.

Best Regards,
Yan, Zi

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)

sentiment_signals: don't think we want to think about
review_comments.raw_body:
I saw this option too, but I wound rather not go this path.

iput() still can lead to inode eviction an bunch of random filesystem
complexity under us. I don't think we want to think about other
fs-related locking issues in split context.

review_comments.author: Zi Yan
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Yan suggested two alternative approaches to avoid rejecting splits: one involves adjusting the lock_at_index based on the split type and offset, while the other uses a new function split_lock_at_index to calculate a safe index for locking.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the order of unlocking and freeing after-split folios, agreeing that i_mmap_unlock_read() should be moved out of the window to avoid slab-use-after-free bugs. The author plans to restructure in v3.
review_comments.raw_body:
Right. With the -EBUSY condition growing this hairy -- and having to stay
correct for non-uniform splits too -- just moving i_mmap_unlock_read() out
of the window looks more attractive.

This is really Hao's original patch with the reasoning corrected, so I kept
him as author. v3 below.

----------------------------------------------------------------------

From: "Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)" <kas@kernel.org>
Subject: [PATCH v3] mm/huge_memory: unlock i_mmap_rwsem before releasing
 after-split folios

__folio_split() keeps dereferencing the mapping after the split:
shmem_uncharge(mapping->host) and remap_page() while the folios are still
frozen/locked, and i_mmap_unlock_read(mapping) at the very end, after the
after-split folios have been unlocked and freed.

Nothing holds an inode reference across that. The split relies on @folio
-- which the beyond-EOF drop loop never removes, as it starts at
folio_next(folio) -- staying locked and in the page cache to hold off
eviction. But the unlock loop unlocks @folio before i_mmap_unlock_read()
runs. If the caller's @lock_at is a tail beyond EOF, as memory_failure()
passes when splitting a poisoned tail of a shmem THP that reaches past
i_size during truncation, it too is gone from the page cache; so once
@folio is unlocked no locked, in-cache folio pins the inode, and a
concurrent final iput() can evict and RCU-free it before
i_mmap_unlock_read() touches i_mmap_rwsem:

  BUG: KASAN: slab-use-after-free in __up_read+0x634/0x790
   i_mmap_unlock_read include/linux/fs.h:537 [inline]
   __folio_split+0x732/0x1640 mm/huge_memory.c:4100
   try_to_split_thp_page+0xab/0x390 mm/memory-failure.c:1675
   memory_failure+0x1394/0x26e0 mm/memory-failure.c:2470

  Freed by task 4601:
   shmem_free_in_core_inode+0x54/0xb0 mm/shmem.c:5177
   evict+0x57f/0xac0 fs/inode.c:870

Do every mapping dereference while @folio still pins the inode: drop
i_mmap_rwsem right after remap_page(), before the loop that unlocks and
frees the after-split folios, and clear @mapping so the exit path does not
unlock it again. shmem_uncharge() and remap_page() already run before that
point, so after this nothing past the unlock loop touches the inode or the
mapping.

This is now a rule the split depends on, alongside keeping @folio frozen
until the page cache is updated: no inode or mapping dereference once the
after-split folios start being unlocked.

Reported-by: Hao Zhang <zhanghao1@kylinos.cn>
Closes: https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260710071344.GA106129@zh-pc
Fixes: baa355fd3314 ("thp: file pages support for split_huge_page()")
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Co-developed-by: Hao Zhang <zhanghao1@kylinos.cn>
Signed-off-by: Hao Zhang <zhanghao1@kylinos.cn>
Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) <kas@kernel.org>
---
 mm/huge_memory.c | 12 ++++++++++++
 1 file changed, 12 insertions(+)

diff --git a/mm/huge_memory.c b/mm/huge_memory.c
index 2bccb0a53a0a..abaea34ef558 100644
--- a/mm/huge_memory.c
+++ b/mm/huge_memory.c
@@ -4109,6 +4109,18 @@ static int __folio_split(struct folio *folio, unsigned int new_order,

 	remap_page(folio, 1 << old_order, ttu_flags);

+	/*
+	 * Drop the mapping while the inode is still pinned. @folio stays
+	 * locked and present in the page cache until the loop below, so
+	 * eviction cannot free the inode yet; @lock_at is not enough, it may
+	 * be a tail beyond EOF that the split already dropped from the page
+	 * cache. Nothing past this point may touch the inode or the mapping.
+	 */
+	if (mapping) {
+		i_mmap_unlock_read(mapping);
+		mapping = NULL;
+	}
+
 	/*
 	 * Unlock all after-split folios except the one containing
 	 * @lock_at page. If @folio is not split, it will be kept locked.
-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/5] mm/huge_memory: refuse to split a file folio when the anchor is beyond EOF
series_items.patch_summary: This patch prevents the Linux kernel from splitting a file folio when its anchor is beyond the end of the file (EOF). The split operation relies on the caller's locked folio to keep the inode alive, but if the anchor lies beyond EOF, it can cause the inode to be evicted and freed while still being used by the split operation. To prevent this, the patch checks if the anchor is at or beyond EOF and returns an error (-EBUSY) in such cases. This fix ensures that the split operation only proceeds when the anchor is within the valid range of the file.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 3/5] mm/huge_memory: remove unused split_huge_page_to_order()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the unused function split_huge_page_to_order() from the Linux kernel's huge_memory module. The function was made redundant by a previous change that converted its last caller to use folio-native functions. As a result, there are no users of this function left in the codebase, and it can be safely removed without any functional changes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 4/5] mm/huge_memory: remove unused can_split_folio()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes the unused function can_split_folio() from the Linux kernel's huge_memory module. The function was left behind by an earlier cleanup and has no callers or definition when CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE is not enabled. Removing it does not introduce any functional changes to the kernel.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 5/5] mm/huge_memory: fold split_folio_to_list_to_order() into split_folio_to_order()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch simplifies the code by removing an unnecessary function and directly calling another function from a third function. The removed function, split_folio_to_list_to_order(), had no direct callers and was only used by another function that always passed NULL as its list argument. This change does not introduce any functional changes to the kernel.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch set fixes a use-after-free (UAF) bug in the Linux kernel's inode handling when splitting a file folio past the end of file (EOF). The bug occurs because the inode is evicted from memory while still being accessed by the split function. To fix this, the patch changes the way memory_failure() splits files: instead of locking and splitting at the tail anchor, it now locks and splits at the head anchor, which remains in the page cache and keeps the inode pinned. Additionally, the patch makes it explicit that a file folio cannot be split when the anchor is beyond EOF, preventing future callers from reintroducing the bug. The changes are tagged for stable and have been compile- and boot-tested.

review_comments.author: Kiryl Shutsemau (author)
review_comments.summary: Author addressed a concern about the order of unlocking and freeing after-split folios, agreeing that moving i_mmap_unlock_read() out of the window is more attractive to avoid complex -EBUSY condition. In v3, they restructured __folio_split() to drop i_mmap_rwsem right after remap_page(), before unlocking and freeing the after-split folios.
review_comments.raw_body:
Right. With the -EBUSY condition growing this hairy -- and having to stay
correct for non-uniform splits too -- just moving i_mmap_unlock_read() out
of the window looks more attractive.

This is really Hao's original patch with the reasoning corrected, so I kept
him as author. v3 below.

----------------------------------------------------------------------

From: "Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta)" <kas@kernel.org>
Subject: [PATCH v3] mm/huge_memory: unlock i_mmap_rwsem before releasing
 after-split folios

__folio_split() keeps dereferencing the mapping after the split:
shmem_uncharge(mapping->host) and remap_page() while the folios are still
frozen/locked, and i_mmap_unlock_read(mapping) at the very end, after the
after-split folios have been unlocked and freed.

Nothing holds an inode reference across that. The split relies on @folio
-- which the beyond-EOF drop loop never removes, as it starts at
folio_next(folio) -- staying locked and in the page cache to hold off
eviction. But the unlock loop unlocks @folio before i_mmap_unlock_read()
runs. If the caller's @lock_at is a tail beyond EOF, as memory_failure()
passes when splitting a poisoned tail of a shmem THP that reaches past
i_size during truncation, it too is gone from the page cache; so once
@folio is unlocked no locked, in-cache folio pins the inode, and a
concurrent final iput() can evict and RCU-free it before
i_mmap_unlock_read() touches i_mmap_rwsem:

  BUG: KASAN: slab-use-after-free in __up_read+0x634/0x790
   i_mmap_unlock_read include/linux/fs.h:537 [inline]
   __folio_split+0x732/0x1640 mm/huge_memory.c:4100
   try_to_split_thp_page+0xab/0x390 mm/memory-failure.c:1675
   memory_failure+0x1394/0x26e0 mm/memory-failure.c:2470

  Freed by task 4601:
   shmem_free_in_core_inode+0x54/0xb0 mm/shmem.c:5177
   evict+0x57f/0xac0 fs/inode.c:870

Do every mapping dereference while @folio still pins the inode: drop
i_mmap_rwsem right after remap_page(), before the loop that unlocks and
frees the after-split folios, and clear @mapping so the exit path does not
unlock it again. shmem_uncharge() and remap_page() already run before that
point, so after this nothing past the unlock loop touches the inode or the
mapping.

This is now a rule the split depends on, alongside keeping @folio frozen
until the page cache is updated: no inode or mapping dereference once the
after-split folios start being unlocked.

Reported-by: Hao Zhang <zhanghao1@kylinos.cn>
Closes: https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20260710071344.GA106129@zh-pc
Fixes: baa355fd3314 ("thp: file pages support for split_huge_page()")
Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org>
Co-developed-by: Hao Zhang <zhanghao1@kylinos.cn>
Signed-off-by: Hao Zhang <zhanghao1@kylinos.cn>
Signed-off-by: Kiryl Shutsemau (Meta) <kas@kernel.org>
---
 mm/huge_memory.c | 12 ++++++++++++
 1 file changed, 12 insertions(+)

diff --git a/mm/huge_memory.c b/mm/huge_memory.c
index 2bccb0a53a0a..abaea34ef558 100644
--- a/mm/huge_memory.c
+++ b/mm/huge_memory.c
@@ -4109,6 +4109,18 @@ static int __folio_split(struct folio *folio, unsigned int new_order,

 	remap_page(folio, 1 << old_order, ttu_flags);

+	/*
+	 * Drop the mapping while the inode is still pinned. @folio stays
+	 * locked and present in the page cache until the loop below, so
+	 * eviction cannot free the inode yet; @lock_at is not enough, it may
+	 * be a tail beyond EOF that the split already dropped from the page
+	 * cache. Nothing past this point may touch the inode or the mapping.
+	 */
+	if (mapping) {
+		i_mmap_unlock_read(mapping);
+		mapping = NULL;
+	}
+
 	/*
 	 * Unlock all after-split folios except the one containing
 	 * @lock_at page. If @folio is not split, it will be kept locked.
-- 
  Kiryl Shutsemau / Kirill A. Shutemov

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v5 2/2] selftests/mm: add PAGEMAP_SCAN test for THP PMD holes
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch adds a test to the selftests/mm suite to ensure that PAGEMAP_SCAN correctly reports written state for PMD holes. The test creates a MAP_PRIVATE|MAP_ANON THP, uffd-wp's it, and then drops it with MADV_DONTNEED, leaving a pmd_none hole without a page table. The patch also includes a helper function to factor out the populate/drop/scan sequence from an existing test.
